steps.title: Support the increase claim
steps.content: The underlined claim says that because of 'Aha Pūnana Leo, the number of young people who speak Hawaiian has increased. Strong support must show both parts: a rise in the number of young speakers and a direct connection between that rise and the school.

steps.title: Use before-and-after counts
steps.content: Choice A gives exactly that kind of evidence: fewer than fifty children could speak Hawaiian when the school was founded, but now more than 2,000 students at the school speak it. Choice B gives only a general Native Hawaiian population statistic. Choice C describes similarities between Hawaiian and other Polynesian languages, and Choice D describes other Pacific Islander communities in Hawai'i. None of those shows a rise in young Hawaiian speakers caused by the school. The answer is A.
